---
title: "General Introduction"
output-file: "01_introduction"
---

# Potato: a very brief history

Potato (_Solanum tuberosum_) has transformed the diet of billions of people over the last century. Cultivated for its starchy tubers, this vegetable has become the most important non-cereal food crop making it a keystone in global food security [@Zaheer2016]. Originating from modern-day Peru, potato has a complex evolutionary history which is still under intense scrutiny. Tuber bearing _Solanum_ species (known as _Petota_), represent one of the most diverse sections within the genus, comprising over 200 species with varying levels of ploidy and ecological adaptations [@Spooner2014]. Only recently has the potato lineage been found to have originated as an inter-specific hybrid between Etuberosum and tomato lineages upwards of 9 million years ago [@Zhang2025]. Neither of these ancestral species were tuber-bearing, and only through subsequent evolutionary events did tuber formation emerge as a key adaptation for reproduction in high-altitude environments [@Hardigan2017]. This hybridization event precipitated an unprecedented radiation resulting in over 100 tuber-bearing species distributed across the Andes stretching from modern-day Colombia to Bolivia [@Spooner2014]. This diversification was possible through the incredible adaptation of these species to multiple climates and ecological niches, ranging from high-altitude páramo ecosystems to subtropical valleys.

Domestication of potato is thought to have originated around 10,000 years ago in the Lake Titicaca region of the Andes, but like its early history, aspects of potato's domestication are rather muddled [@Spooner2005]. There are two primary origin theories, namely, a single domestication event from _Solanum bukasovii_ or multiple domestication events from different wild progenitors. The impact of domestication on potato did not only result in differences in the harvested tubers (e.g. enlargement of tubers, reduction of anti-nutrients content like glycoalkaloids, and changes in tuber shape and skin color), but also altered flowering patterns, photoperiod sensitivity, and reproductive biology [@Hardigan2017]. Through domestication, potato evolved from a short-day adapted plant to one with greater environmental plasticity. The introduction of tetraploid Andean landraces to Europe in the mid-16^th^ century marked the beginning of potato cultivation in European agriculture. It was not until the introduction and admixture with Chilean potato landraces that potato was adapted to long-day conditions, leading to the widespread adoption and spread of potato across Europe [@DeJong2016; @Gutaker2019].

The origins of selective efforts of potato in Europe, like many crops, were quite rudimentary. Just as potato was being adopted by the peasantry in England and Ireland in the mid-18^th^ century, a few prescient observations were made around their propagation. The growing of potato seedlings over seed tubers was thought to buffer the deterioration observed in extant clones, perhaps observing the accumulation of pathogen load over multiple clonal generations [@Glendinning1983a]. Additionally, while a few different "_types_" of potato were observed to be grown in Britain, the reliance on clonal propagation meant that European potato agriculture was one of the first true genetic monocultures with devastating consequences. Between 1845 and 1849, Europe experienced one of the worst famines in modern history. Due to a continent-wide _Phytophthora infestans_ epidemic, further compounded by an exploitive farmer tenant system that encouraged nearly exclusive potato cultivation, catastrophic crop failure fell upon Ireland. The resulting Great Hunger in Ireland and related Continental Famine in Europe was responsible for an excess deaths of over 1.1 million people [@Zadoks2008]. While the exact causes of the blight were not rightly known at the time, it was generally understood that there a _deficiency_ in the majority of European potato and that any human dependence on it necessitated immediate improvement in the crop.

# Modern Potato breeding

By the 19^th^ century, potato cultivation was expanding across North America. Moreover, intentional crosses and selection of well-adapted seedlings was becoming commonplace leading to the creation of multiple varieties in the latter decades preceding the 20^th^ century [@Stevenson1937]. This period of breeding was prolific and marked by the influx of exotic germplasm from Chile such as Rough purple Chili (~1850) or Daber (~1860) [@Glendinning1983a; @Plaisted1989]. The progeny of these varieties have been so valuable that they continue to be cultivated even now; these include varieties such as Russet Burbank (1908), King Edward (1902), and Bintje (1910) [@VanBerloo2007]. These new varieties were not only carrying valuable resistances to blight and scab, but were also being bred for their valuable starch to satisfy the burgeoning North American and European fermentation and processing industries [@Willard1993]. Potato's newfound industrial capacity was taking place just as its popularity in its fried form, "_pommes frittes_", was being discovered on a new world stage. Already by the early 1920s, potato production was feeding a multi-faceted global economy as it was being formed in real time.

Once the province of hobbyists, selection of novel and better potato varieties began rapid formalization as breeders began to select for well-adapted clones through the early 20^th^ century. By 1950, public breeding institutions were founded in the United States (USDA breeding institutes; 1910), Britain (Scotland's plant breeding station; 1920), India (Potato Breeding Station; 1935), and China (1940) [@Luthra2006; @Jansky2009; @Bradshaw2009; @Jansky2018]. For many of these institutions, the primary end was adapting potato to important production locations, but also for the improvement of traits like maturity, marketable yield fractions, insect and disease resistance, external tuber quality (e.g. eye depth, skin cracking), and cooking quality to name a few [@Krantz1946; @Douches1996]. In parallel to this, international gene banks were established and charged not only with the task of taxonomical classification, but also assessing the genetic utility of these collections. The International Potato Center (a member of the Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research centers) was one of the largest of these institutions but other notable germplasm centers includes the United States Potato Introduction Station, BrACySol (INRAE), and the Vavilov Institute of Plant Genetic Resources [@Huaman1986; @Bamberg1994; @Dalamu2025]. These centers have become invaluable to modern potato improvement through their identification of novel abiotic and biotic resistances among exotic germplasm.

```{r}
#| fig.cap: "Binned number of released cultivars since 1750 where the year of release was known (van Berloo et al., 2007)."
#| label: "fig-varieties"
#| warning: false
#| echo: false
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
getThese <- c(
  twister = "Twister",
  bintje = "Bintje",
  `yukon gold` = "Yukon Gold",
  innovator = "Innovator",
  `reading russet` = "Russet",
  `rough purple chili` = "Rough Purple Chili"
)
dat <- fread("./tbl.tsv")[, .(name, year)]
#doing this stepwise
dat[, year := as.numeric(gsub("<|>", "", year))][,
  name := gsub("find progeny of (.*)\\s{3,10}.*$", "\\1", name)
][, name := trimws(tolower(name))]
dit <- unique(dat[!is.na(year)])
dit[, Name := getThese[name]]
setorder(dit, year)
coords <- c(200, 300, 400, -20, -60, -100)
ggplot(dit, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_histogram(color = "black", fill = "#005887") +
  geom_segment(
    data = dit[!is.na(Name)],
    aes(y = coords, yend = 0, xend = year, x = year),
    color = "black",
    lty = 2
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = dit[!is.na(Name)],
    aes(y = coords),
    size = 4,
    fill = "red",
    pch = 21
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = dit[!is.na(Name)],
    aes(label = Name, y = coords, x = year),
    angle = 10,
    nudge_x = -5,
    hjust = 1
  ) +
  xlab("Release Year") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ylab("Number of Potato Variety Releases")
```

Over the past 50 years, potato research benefited from rapid methodological advances in both molecular and cell biology. Potato was one of the first crop plants to utilize _in vitro_ propagation and manipulation techniques. This includes the development of protocols related to minituber propagation & virus elimination [@HUSSEY1981; @Jones1994]. Along with having a positive impact on production of clean potato stock, _in vitro_ methods made potato a prime candidate for gene-editing technologies. By the turn of the century, potato was on the forefront of biotechnological improvement with several transgenic clones targeting traits such as insect resistance and amylose fractions [@Thornton2003; @Karlsson2007]. While gene-editing technologies are currently under-utilized among breeders in western Europe, there are many other agronomic and tuber composition traits that would be worthwhile candidates for transformation [@Millam2007]. Perhaps more than any other recent technology, the development and application of molecular markers for simple and complex trait associations have greatly impacted the efficiency of potato breeding [@Gebhardt2007]. Some of the first genetic mapping efforts with restriction and amplified fragment length polymorphisms (RFLP and AFLP, respectively) were responsible for the initial mapping for regions providing resistance to _Phytophthora infestans_, Potato Virus X, and _Globodera rostochiensis_ [@Gebhardt1989; @Gebhardt1993; @Meksem1995]. Almost exclusively, these first genetic maps were developed in diploid mapping populations, but with the advent of the next generation sequencing and the creation of the first reference genome, linkage mapping efforts soon expanded to tetraploid populations [@Bradshaw2008; @ThePotatoGenomeSequencingConsortium2011; @Hackett2013]. This has led to incredible progress in both quantitative trait locus (QTL) mapping methodology as well as genomic prediction opening up many modern data-driven tooling to potato [@Enciso-Rodriguez2018; @Bourke2021; @Pandey2022; @Clot2024b].




::: {.content-hidden}

[@ThePotatoGenomeSequencingConsortium2011], genomic resources have transformed potato breeding. This landmark achievement enabled the development of various molecular tools including:

**Marker-assisted selection (MAS)**: MAS allows breeders to select for desirable traits based on molecular markers linked to genes of interest, particularly useful for traits with low heritability or those difficult to phenotype [@Slater2014].

**QTL mapping and genomic selection**: Quantitative trait locus (QTL) mapping has identified genomic regions associated with important traits, while genomic selection uses genome-wide markers to predict breeding values, accelerating selection cycles [@Endelman2018].

**High-throughput phenotyping**: Automated phenotyping platforms enable more precise and efficient trait measurement, particularly for complex traits like tuber quality and disease resistance [@Cabrera-Bosquet2012].

These technologies also enable optimizations for typical breeding pipelines for clonal potato, reducing cycle times and increasing selection accuracy.

A popular topic of interest has been to exploit the power of unreduced gametes, as a means for inter-ploidy breeding [@Clot2024b]. This approach allows for the transfer of genetic material between different ploidy levels, potentially combining desirable traits from diploid and tetraploid germplasm.

:::


## Challenges in potato genetic improvement

Much of potato's history chronicles difficulties surrounding potato's cultivation. Even incidental aspects of potato's evolution reach through millennia and affect growers with many ramifications on modern production systems. Characteristics of potato's biology also hamper selection and improvement of potato germplasm; these I highlight below.

While species within _Petota_ range widely in ploidy number, cultivated potato is almost entirely tetraploid (2n = 48) exhibiting a tetrasomic pattern of inheritance. Potato's autoploidy naturally has some ramifications for breeding. Polyploidy in crops is often described as a sort of double-edged sword in the context of genetic improvement. Multiple genome copies is generally associated with several benefits in crop species including larger organ size, greater _heterotic_ vigour, and more robust performance to sub-adequate climate conditions [@Osborn2003; @Renny-Byfield2014]. These benefits often arise as a response to genome duplication followed by gene-conversion or gene-neofunctionalization which is thought to be a beneficial mode for positive evolutionary selection. Some researchers would describe _Petota_'s polyploidy as one of the major vehicles for its wide environmental adaptability [@Hijmans2007; @Tai2025].

Despite these benefits in an evolutionary setting, polyploidy through the lens of breeding is generally seen as cumbersome. Gene frequencies in tetraploids are strongly buffered from perturbations, making directional selection slower in a tetraploid system relative to their diploid counterparts [@Osborn2003]. This becomes quite clear when considering a single locus model. Using the single locus model of @Bernardo2002a, we can define the allelic variance at a given locus as the variance of a binomial distribution, i.e.

$$ \sigma^2_p = \frac{p\cdot (1-p)}{l \cdot N} $$

where $p$ is the allele frequency in the population, $l$ is the ploidy number and $N$ is the number of individuals in the population. Comparing two populations (one diploid, $l = 2$, and the other tetraploid, $l = 4$) comprised of 10 individuals ($N = 10$) and an allele frequency of 0.35 ($p = 0.35$), the allelic variance would be 0.011 and 0.0057 in the diploid and tetraploid populations, respectively. Considering how these allelic variances affect the probability of allelic shifts, we can examine the question of a random positive 0.1 shift in the next generation. Evaluating this probability ($P(p \geq 0.45) = 1 - \Phi\left(\frac{0.1}{\sigma_p}\right)$) in both populations results in a probabilities of 0.17 and 0.09 in the diploid and  tetraploid populations, respectively. This demonstrates that gene frequencies even under a simple drift model are buffered from random fluctuations in polyploids relative to their diploid counterparts. This touches on all population dynamics which artificial selection seeks to modulate including inbreeding, single locus selection and fixation, and even population improvement schemas which extend across thousands of loci.

Speed of selection aside, potato also carries with it the burden of deleterious mutation load. Like many other polyploid crops, accumulation of deleterious mutations was a byproduct of a genome duplication event leading to inherent redundancy of multiple genome copies. This genome masking and a predominate clonal propagation strategy resulted in the amassing of mutations as maternal plants passed on their genome to daughter tubers over thousands of generations [@Lian2019]. This process was nearly universal in potato resulting in the hundreds of thousands of functionally deleterious mutations among both landraces and cultivated species. The impact of this evolutionary process is directly observable via inbreeding of tetraploid populations or haploidization of tetraploids leading to immediate loss of vigour, severe segregation distortion, and detectable zygotic and gametic selection signatures [@Zhang2019; @Song2023]. The moment a breeder sexually propagates tetraploid potato and attempts artificial selection among the progeny, the millennia of mutations are then unmasked resulting in incredible phenotypical variances in this first generation.

These large variances observed in segregating tetraploid populations have knock-on effects for artificial selection on tetraploid potato. First, a breeder must generate enough progeny per biparental cross to sufficiently "_sample_" from all possible genetic combinations possible to increase the likelihood of creating a progeny of higher merit for some trait(s). However, this presents a second challenge of _finding_ that progeny of higher merit among hundreds of thousands of seedlings. Like any other field crop, the best means of evaluating a potato clone's merit is through multiple variety trials which would allow for an unbiased evaluation of those traits [@Smith2005]. This on one hand sounds like a simple problem, but this gets into several issues unique to clonal crops. In the initial generations, breeders are reliant on true potato seed (TPS) based evaluation which excludes any possibility of replicated evaluations as these seedlings are being generated from segregating families. More problematic, however, is that phenotypic correlations between initial seedling trials and subsequent clonal generations are considerably low [@Maris1988; @Gu2025]. This limits most phenotypic based selection on tuber and yield characteristics until later clonal generations where sufficient tuber-based propagule is available. The cumulative effect of these factors is a reduced selection accuracy with slower genetic progress relative to other crops [@Gopal2015].

One of the more underappreciated challenges in potato breeding is the sheer number of traits which beset breeders. Depending on the program and market segment, a potato breeder could be considering over 40 traits among a panel of candidates [@Gebhardt2013]. Many of these characteristics also exhibit complex genotype by environment and even genotype by management interactions further necessitating high quality and consistent multi-environment trials. Specific economic traits such as starch content, cold sweetening, and skin firmness are especially sensitive to many post-harvest conditions [@Jansky2006]. This means that trialing of potato extends outside the field trial and includes the assessment of management factors across the haulming, harvest, and tuber storage stages. 

Perhaps the most important category of traits in potato are those related to yield. These are expressed in terms of sheer mass (e.g. Gross Tonnes per hectare, Marketable yield per hectare, Tonnes of starch) or with respect to some component of yield (e.g. Number of tubers per stem, average size of tuber) and are often the direct targets of evaluation and selection [@Tai1976; @Lemaga1990; @Haynes2001]. One of the most pressing questions for potato breeders is with regard to increasing yield in potato and why there appears to be less genetic improvement with respect to yield in other crops over the past century. Part of the complexity in this question is related to the aforementioned large trait surface in potato, but also potato's market segmentation. Because potato is divided into multiple distinct industries (ware, chips, processing, ingredient), there are multiple selection criteria for each which cannot be met with one program. So even if marketable yield had increased for russet types or tuber appearance for the chipping market, these gains are not easily transferable to other segments [@Douches1996; @Hirsch2013]. So while over the past 40 years there has been a rapid  number of varieties being released ([@fig-varieties]), these often reflect improvements in resistances and tuber quality with little attention towards gross yield. This reflects a difficulty transferring traits between different market segments or potato types meaning genetic improvement in potato tends towards stratification.

# Hybrid Potato breeding

Despite many modern advances in potato breeding [@Bethke2019], the traditional clonal breeding strategy has essential barriers that retard the breeding process. Many modifications of this schema have been proposed to circumvent these challenges, but one of particular interest is adapting potato to a hybrid breeding schema [@Lindhout2011; @Jansky2018; @Bradshaw2022]. Potato is certainly not the first crop where this has been considered; maize aside, sugar beet, wheat, barley, and rice are all field crops which have viable F1 hybrid programs [@Crow1998]. The justifications for such an endeavour are well known, but the benefits of hybridization in crops are comprised of factors economical, biological, and logistical. Focusing on the latter two components, hybrid breeding is designed around maximizing the heterotic response among the F1 progeny of inbred lines from opposing pools. While more capital intensive with longer breeding cycles than line breeding, hybrid breeding is characterized by its increased uniformity, exploitation of hybrid vigour, and capacity to quickly develop crop varieties [@Cowling2020; @Labroo2021]. Moreover, a hybrid breeding schema offers particular efficiencies for potato. First, is the direct utilization of true potato seed (TPS) in the trialling and release of cultivars [@Almekinders1996; @Stockem2021]. This leverages TPS as a means of rapid propagule multiplication which further enables replicated field testing earlier in the breeding cycle. This directly affects the quality of phenotypic selection by increasing the heritability and thus allows for selection earlier in the breeding program. Along with this, hybrid breeding designs can be quite conducive for simultaneous improvement across multiple traits, especially through the incorporation of technologies like genomic selection [@Zhao2015; @Peixoto2024]. Hybrid breeding programs are compartmentalized by design and allow for selection targets per pool thus decreasing the complexity of the large trait surface.

Benefits aside, transforming clonal potato into a hybrid crop is a resource intensive process that involves several prerequisite steps. Such an adaptation necessitates a few major measures to render a potential breeding program: (1) Breeding potato at a diploid level and (2) generation of self-compatible and self-fertile germplasm. Regarding ploidy, much has already been written here. Since the mid-20^th^ century, there have been multiple efforts to breed with diploid germplasm. Interest in diploid breeding range from faster inbreeding rates, increased ease of introgressing exotic diploid germplasm, and bridge programs [@Krantz1946; @Hougas1958; @Chase1963]. It should be said that while hybrid breeding is technically possible in autotetraploid potato, it is impractical for many of the aforementioned reasons. The major bottleneck with diploid breeding lies in the process of dihaploidization which is most commonly genome reduction via angiogenesis [@Uijtewaal1987]. The other imperative in hybrid breeding is the ability to rapidly develop pure lines via selfing. Potato, practically an obligate outcrosser, presents a few barriers with its self-incompatibility mechanism being the most significant. Self-incompatibility in potato, while not universal, is widespread in tuber-bearing Solanum predominately in the form of gametophytic self-incompatibility locus, _Sli_ [@Hosaka1998; @Jing2025]. Since its discovery, however, this locus is now one of the primary tools used in research and breeding programs to create self-compatible diploid germplasm [@Ma2021; @Eggers2021]. While this is the primary barrier for inbreeding potato, symptoms of inbreeding depression in potato include fertility anomalies such as bud abortion, lower pollen count, and poor seed yields all of which have ramifications in hybrid breeding programs as well [@Pallais1987]. Despite these challenges, there are (at the time of writing) several active public and private programs with nearly or fully inbred diploid potato populations which are actively under development [@Zhang2021; @Song2023; @DeVries2023].

With many of these biological barriers addressed, we are still left with many questions surrounding the feasibility of hybrid potato programs. There is particularly little known regarding the population or genetic properties of current extant material. This is pertinent as there are few factors more relevant to the viability of a hybrid breeding program than evaluating the current status of complex traits. This includes the following: a thorough examination of heterotic vigour, current yield potential, and an assessment of relevant genetic models applicable to inference and prediction of field performance of hybrid potato. As we have already addressed, hybrid breeding should theoretically provide a means of monitoring and exploiting heterotic vigour as a breeding program progresses over time; if this vigour is less than expectation, then one of the primary benefits of such a system is unfulfilled for potato. Related to this, utilizing biometrical methods, we should be able to estimate accurate genetic parameters for yield components which offer both a vetting of the current capacity of F1 hybrids but also a forecast of the future potential of such programs [@Villanueva1993]. Lastly, the creation of a new novel crop offers an opportunity to appraise the utility of novel biometrical and predictive methodologies for the prediction of genetic merit in a young hybrid breeding program.

# Aim of this thesis

There remains a significant gap in comprehensive biometrical analyses of its genetic architecture and the application of genomic methods in hybrid potato. With the exception of a handful of published studies [@Jansky2018; @Lindhout2011; @Endelman2018], there have been few quantitative inspections of the genetic architecture or surveys of genomic methods applied to hybrid potato. This thesis aims to address this gap through two primary directives. First, I wish to apply a battery of traditional genetic models successfully leveraged in other hybrid crops to evaluate the performance of a F1 hybrid potato and assess genetic mechanisms underlying multiple yield-related phenotypes. Second, I seek to use this material to evaluate modern predictive modeling frameworks to determine which technologies are most effective for hybrid potato breeding programs. The layout if this of thesis is as follows:

**Chapter 2** begins with the evaluation of an F1 hybrid multi-environmental trial (MET). This chapter focuses primarily on estimating intra-trial spatial variation and statistically delineating several genetic components of variation, namely additive and non-additive genetic effects operating within this hybrid crossing block. I consider how these genetic fractions for several tuber traits might impact breeding strategies and selection decisions.

**Chapter 3** examines the potential of multiple METs for training genome-wide regression models. This analysis is conducted on several heritable traits, with specific attention to different genetic parameterizations to assess the prediction accuracy of each model. The genomic models tested are analogous to those examined in **Chapter 2**, with the addition of a model validation procedure specifically designed for F1 hybrid datasets.

**Chapter 4** provides a broader evaluation of genomic models and assesses their potential in several selection scenarios. Specifically, the efficiency of genomic selection is contrasted with marker-assisted selection (MAS) schemas based on putative quantitative trait loci (QTL) in a panel of F1 hybrids. The primary aim is to evaluate whether quantitative trait improvement in diploid hybrid potato could be more efficient with MAS-based procedures compared to genomic selection approaches.

**Chapter 5** addresses the question of molecular marker information and whether multiallelic marker data offers any utility in predictive applications. Two forms of multiallelic information are considered: short-range haplotypes (or haplotags) and founder-origin variants (or identity-by-descent based variants). I examine the utility of both forms of multiallelism through a broad survey of predictive genomic models to assess their benefits over traditional SNP-based predictors.

**Chapter 6** concludes with a synthesis of the previous experimental chapters, with particular emphasis on how these findings advance biometrical applications in hybrid potato. We conclude by offering a survey of currently under-served research topics and discussing their relevance to hybrid potato breeding systems, providing direction for future investigations in this emerging field.


# References


::: {.content-hidden}
# Introduction


# Section on Breeding 4.0

__Steps with domestication/formalisation of breeding a crop__

This conversion of tetraploid clonal potato into a diploid inbred-hybrid crop can be considered a realisation of a wider effort in crop breeding denoted re-domestication or _de novo_ domestication, wherein novel or related species outside a conventional gene pool are rapidly bred usually through targeting of known domestication genes and pathways via genome editing [(Xie &Liu, 2021)](https://academic.oup.com/nsr/article/8/4/nwab033/6152298). This is perhaps best exemplified in (Yu et al., 2021) where a process for allotetraploid rice is laid out. 

_De novo_ domestication itself is a facet in a larger movement in breeding described as 4^th^ generation breeding [(Fernie & Yan, 2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1674205219301297) also known as Breeding 4.0 within some in the quantitative genetic community [(Wallace, Rodgers-Melnick, & Buckler, 2018)](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jason-Wallace-6/publication/328085929_On_the_Road_to_Breeding_40_Unraveling_the_Good_the_Bad_and_the_Boring_of_Crop_Quantitative_Genomics/links/602bca0392851c4ed577af51/On-the-Road-to-Breeding-40-Unraveling-the-Good-the-Bad-and-the-Boring-of-Crop-Quantitative-Genomics.pdf).

# Notes

## Genetics, Genomicos and breeding of potato

 - Higher calorie per hectare than cereals
 - Crossability controlled by endosperm balance 
 - Consumed for at least 13,000 years (Ugent et al. 1987)
 - Likely single domestication event Spooner 2005
 - 200 tuber bearing potato species in section Petota
 - Vast genetic resources among so-called tertiary gene pools
 
 
 - 840 MBp
 - Ro locus tuber shape 1994
 

:::
